(3) Circulation of financial resources. It refers to the rational allocation and reuse of economic resources, which is the most essential application of the financial system. However, the efficiency of allocation and reuse of economic resources is often affected by the complexity of economic transactions. In the financial framework under the background of the present international financial system, it has become possible to allocate, use and reuse economic resources across countries.

(4) Risk control of financial system. Professional financial services mostly deal with the risk control of economic resources, such as options trading services. Essentially, the circulation of funds in financial transactions will inevitably generate risks, so the establishment of the financial system will surely provide a variety of controls for predictable risks.

(5) Information supply. In the process of financial transactions, the supply of price information can often offer some reference for the participants of market transactions, thereby reducing the loss resulting from the lack of information. The effective functioning of information supply can not only provide data support for financial institutions in risk control but also make financial transactions more transparent.

(6) Solving incentive problems. Incentive problems are bound to affect other functions of the financial system and relevant financial institutions in resource allocation, transfer of resources, risk management, etc. The consequences, for instance, include adverse selection between principals and agents and moral hazard. Especially when there exists information asymmetry in the market, the financial system can provide relevant solutions to incentive problems.
Interrelationships among Rural Finance, Financial Inclusion and Digital Finance

In this dissertation, we analyze the logical relationship among rural finance, financial inclusion and digital finance, and discuss the feasibility of developing rural inclusive finance via digitalization.
Relation between Rural Finance and Financial Inclusion

Financial inclusion is a concept and overall framework intended to resolve financial exclusion and eradicate financial discrimination. Financial exclusion is the term coined for the poor and unprivileged groups lacking access to mainstream financial services at affordable costs. The factors underlying financial exclusion may include geographical, economic, social and financial service market factors. Geographical exclusion refers to the fact that the excluded subjects are far away from financial service institutions and need public transport to get to financial service institutions;

economic exclusion means mainstream financial institutions shut those engaged in high-risk and low-income operations outside through industry risk assessment and otherwise; social exclusion means mainstream financial institutions exclude economic participants using certain marketing strategies or the excluded subjects themselves believe that there is only a slim chance for them to obtain financial services even if they submit applications in view of their experience and psychological factors, think that they are virtually excluded from financial services.

Rural areas, agriculture and farmers are the key service objects of rural finance. Rural areas are remote and far away from financial institutions, and long-distance transportation is needed before reaching them, falling into the category of geographical exclusion; agriculture is a high-risk and low-return industry, falling into the category of economic exclusion; farmers are less educated and find it hard to afford financial services, falling into the category of social exclusion. The above analysis shows that the service objects of rural finance are basically the objects of financial exclusion. The main goal of financial inclusion is to eradicate financial exclusion, including, of course, to solve the financial inclusion problem of “agriculture, rural areas and farmers”, that is, to solve the current problems of rural finance. Therefore, financial inclusion provides conception and basic framework for solving rural financial problems.
To sum up, financial inclusion is a concept targeting at solving rural financial problems. And financial problems in underdeveloped rural areas are an important aspect of financial inclusion.
3.3.2 Relation
between Rural Finance and Digital Technology

1. Digital Integration into Finance: One of the core features of the early digital transformation of rural financial institutions is the digitization of financial services and business channels. The rapid development of financial technology has driven innovation in rural financial services, with digital technology gradually shifting from supporting to leading these services. In the process of digital transformation, digital technology is embedded throughout the entire process of financial services and deeply integrated with finance. This results in digitally-based transaction scenarios, data-driven analysis and exploration, as well as comprehensive digital credit management mechanisms.

2. Finance Embedded into Digital: In the digital era of the economy, financial competition is about financial ecosystems and data platforms. During the process of digital transformation, finance is integrated into various aspects of people's daily lives, creating a new format of scenario-based finance, providing people with ecological scenario financial services. Therefore, the digital transformation of rural financial institutions is not merely the adoption of digital technology but rather a comprehensive, mutually empowering process between the organization and technology. As digital transformation advances, digital technology gradually extends into the strategic planning, organizational structure, business models, product design, operational mechanisms, and IT systems of rural financial institutions, making digitalization a fundamental aspect of rural finance. In the process of the mutual embedding of digital technology and rural finance, a digital financial ecosystem gradually takes shape.

Based on the analysis above, we can see that rural finance falls within the important category of inclusive finance, which can help improve the underdeveloped state of rural finance. However, the specific approach requires further discussion. The application of digitalization is one of the practical pathways for inclusive finance and has already proven its inclusivity in urban financial development. Whether digitalization can solve rural financial issues becomes a crucial question. Logically, it is feasible, primarily for the following reasons:

The fundamental difference between rural and urban areas lies in "agriculture, rural areas, and farmers." Digital technology can effectively accommodate this difference. It can aggregate small, dispersed rural areas online, consolidate and analyze data and information that is difficult to collect, and create a "small world" on digital platforms that is no different from urban areas.
Agriculture represents a significant difference. The application of digitalization in urban finance has
demonstrated its effectiveness for the "long tail" market, and agriculture falls into this category, making it compatible with digital solutions.

Farmer differences: Digital finance not only brings financial services but also leads farmers to enjoy the benefits of information technology, broadening their horizons and improving their overall financial literacy.
In summary, digital applications can, in macrological terms, enhance the inclusiveness of rural finance.
3.4 Inevitability for the Digitalization Entry into Rural Financial Market
3.4.1 Theoretical Basis for the Digital Technology’s Role in Promoting Rural Financial Inclusion

Branchless banking is an important means to achieve financial inclusion. Branchless banking is defined as the strategy of delivering financial services outside conventional bank branches. On the one hand, this strategy may be complementary to the functions of existing banking branches, and collaboration between the two may broaden the channel of financial services; on the other hand, branchless banking may exist as a channel independent from conventional banking branches, completely replacing and getting rid of the mode of delivering financial services based on physical branches. Branchless banking technologies include the digital banking, ATM, POS, EFTPOS, mobile banking and so on. A survey of 2011 showed that 62% of respondents preferred the digital banking as their bank service channel, while only 20% chose the traditional mode of banking outlet service (in 2007, 40% preferred the mode of banking outlet service) (PwC, 2012). Major reasons behind that include the improvement of customer digitalization and the increase of infrastructure cost for physical branches.

Application of digital information technology or information and communication technology has become a very important contributor of financial inclusion (Gupta, 2011). In recent years, many developing countries have made use of information and communication technologies (ICTs) to innovate the means and forms of financial service delivery, effectively expanding the reach of financial services to remote and poor people and increasing the accessibility of financial services to unprivileged groups. The concept and models of e-financial Inclusion were introduced in such context. ICT-based financial innovation can reduce the cost and improve the convenience of access to financial services. Mobile finance plays an increasingly prominent role in accelerating financial inclusion and economic growth. For example, a study on African countries was conducted using the data of 1988-2007 to analyze the impact of mobile phone development on financial inclusion and economic growth. The results showed that mobile phone development imposes a significant positive impact on economic growth because, among other reasons, the mobile phone development facilitates financial inclusion (Andrianaivo and Kpodar, 2012). Another study reported that in Colombia, a South American country with underdeveloped finance, the government and the private sector have developed many channels in an attempt to reduce the transaction cost of financial services, improve the availability of financial services and achieve financial inclusion, including financial services using the Internet, voice mail, mobile phones, bank agents, etc. An important means is to facilitate delivery of financial services using mobile banking (Fernndez de Lis, 2014).

A report published by Dun & Bradstreet in 2014 pointed out that increased use of the Internet in financial services would help increase transparency and reduce distribution costs. According to the data of D&B survey, 84% of the respondents preferred the Internet as the main channel of financial service delivery. The Internet helps to improve the accessibility of financial services (expanding coverage), enhance operational efficiency and reduce costs. The impacts of the Internet on financial inclusion are reflected in two aspects. The first is the transparency of product prices and features (product publicity and presentation). Second, the use of the digital technology can increase market segmentation and product segregation so that financial service providers can design different
products for different customer groups, thereby reducing distribution and delivery costs.

In fact, some large credit rating agencies have become increasingly concerned with the problem of how to apply digital technologies in mining of rural big data so as to provide credit rating services for rural lending activities.

For example, the Policy and Economic Research Council (PERC) of the United States and Experian MicroAnalytics (EMA) worked together to develop the Financial Identity Risk Management (FIRM), an innovative scheme for improving the accessibility of financial services in emerging markets (especially the rural poor populations), in a hope to improve the data needed for credit risk assessment and borrowing capacity appraisal.
The Issue 76 (Feb. 2014) of
ICT Update

, an E-journal focusing on ICTs, agriculture and rural development in Africa, the Caribbean and the Pacific, set the topic as mobile finance and agriculture, stating that mobile finance would revolutionize the finance of the agricultural supply chain (ICT Update, 2014). An article titled “Big data for small holder” was devoted to introducing FIRM.

The development of digital technology and the improvement of rural financial informatization can reduce the cost of information collection in financial services, alleviate the problem of information asymmetry, and facilitate the delivery of credit services. The advancement of information technology and its application in financial innovation can reduce the operational costs of financial services delivered by banks and enable the banks to increase services, especially in remote areas. ICTs have a wide range of applications in financial innovation. We can examine and comprehend the impact of information technology on financial innovation in developing countries and relevant rationales from the aspects of credit system construction, branchless banking, innovation of payment means and improvement of payment environment. The rationales for CITs to improve rural financial service innovation and financial supply and to promote financial service innovation and expand service coverage in remote areas mainly includes the following aspects.

Firstly, the development of digital technology can facilitate the establishment and improvement of the credit information system, improve the information environment for credit decision-making of rural financial institutions, and enhance the efficiency of credit decision-making. Meanwhile, financial institutions can adopt information technology to identify, measure and control credit risks and improve risk management capabilities. Secondly, IT-based development of financial products such as e-finance, digital finance and mobile banking is conducive to expanding the service coverage in geographically remote rural areas and promoting rural financial inclusion. Thirdly, financial institutions can use information technology to improve the information system and enhance internal management efficiency, especially the vertical management ability, so as to facilitate sinking of service delivery channels. Fourthly, financial development backed by electronic information technology can cement rural financial infrastructure, improve the construction of rural payment system and payment environment, and facilitate the delivery of rural financial services.

In addition, the digital technology can not only facilitate financial services but also further increase the sources of funding in rural markets. At present, financing in the countryside of China mainly relies on endogenous financing channels. That means, capitals are accumulated from inside. But this financing channel has great shortcomings, seriously impeding the expanded reproduction of rural economy. The acceleration of agricultural modernization poses stricter requirements for the efficiency and quality of agricultural production. With the constant development and upgrading of science and technology, outdated production equipment and technologies have been replaced, and new equipment and technologies have been used in agricultural production. However, the shortage of funding for introducing these new equipment and technologies has become a problem demanding prompt solution in rural development. Since the reform and opening-up, China’s rural land circulation and urbanization have been advancing steadily, and the government’s support and guiding policies such as “Bringing Culture/Science and Technology/Hygienic Health to the

Countryside” and “college-graduate village officials” have greatly improved the overall quality of farmers. Agricultural modernization is speeding up and rural economy shows huge potentials, leading to enormous financing demands. All these factors will create golden opportunities for the development of rural financial market.

Digitalization provides the rural financial market with a financing channel featuring a relatively low threshold, convenient and fast services and relatively fair and equal financing opportunities. Innovative crowdfunding, P2P lending and other credit models also increase the sources of capital in the rural financial market. Digital finance not only plays a positive role in alleviating financing difficulties in rural areas but also has a profound impact on driving the modernization of agricultural industry. It leverages the remarkable advantages of third-party payment platforms of electronic commerce to speed up circulation of agricultural products. The Chinese government strongly advocates the digital finance business model, which will further the connection between rural digital finance and rural economy and make digital finance a powerful engine driving the rural economic development.
3.4.2 Cases Illustrating the Digital Technology’s Role in Promoting Rural Financial Inclusion
(I) African cases: M-PESA in Kenya and MTN in Uganda

In Africa, most of the population have no access to financial services. Only 20% of African households have bank accounts. For example, in Ethiopia, Uganda and Tanzania, a bank offers financial services to 100,000 people on average. Moreover, even those who have access to the banking services often complaint about their inconvenience in view of high transaction costs (Ondiege, 2010). Facing this situation, African countries have been alleviating financial exclusion by developing mobile banking, mobile wallet and other products and services in the past few years. Most notably, Kenya-based Safaricom introduced mobile banking and POS terminal services to successfully launch the M-PESA service program. The target customers of M-PESA are those having no access to banking services and no bank accounts. Also, M-PESA was introduced to Tanzania by Vodacom.

In Uganda, MTN introduced the card-based mobile wallet. The service is to help customers with deposit operations through 1,300 agents. Customers will receive text messages prompting that the deposit has been posted, and then they can transfer the money to other mobile banking users.
(II) Asian cases: True Move in Thailand

In Thailand, the mobile operator True has contributed to the development of mobile banking. Bank of Thailand supports the use of mobile money considering that the management cost of cash is too high. Those having no access to banking services in Thailand are mostly unregistered workers and refugees in shelters. Mobile money is mostly used in mobile recharge, fund transfer, bill payment and purchase of goods, but most of the users are young people. Thanks to the development of mobile banking, Thailand’s financial services have been increasing substantially in recent years.
(III) Latin American cases: Tigo and Personal in Paraguay

Most Latin American countries are facing the problems of scattered banking outlets and inadequate financial infrastructure. Paraguay, for example, is one of the poorest countries in Latin America, with serious financial inequality and insufficient access to education and financial services in rural areas. Bank branches are most sparsely distributed in Paraguay among all other countries in Central and South America, with only five branches serving 100,000 people on average. Even counting some informal financial institutions, 70% of Paraguay’s population is still excluded from financial services. However, the penetration of mobile phones in Latin America has hit 100%. In recent years, innovations in digital technology have increased the access to financial services. Tigo and Personal, Latin America’s two largest telecom operators, are promoting the development of mobile wallet platforms. In Paraguay, Tigo secures 54.67% shares of the mobile phone market. In 2008, Tigo was

the first to introduce the mobile wallet Tigo Cash that is mainly used for retail payment. The registration is done by verifying with the agent outlets, and the transaction is handled by transferring money from E-wallets. In 2010, Tigo developed and launched Giros Tigo to deliver
Chinese

transfer services. Customer A remits mobile money at a designated branch, and then customer B who has received a text message prompt goes to the branch to collect money with his PIN code. Personal, Paraguay’s second largest telecom operator, maintains 29.68% shares of the mobile phone market. In 2010, it began to introduce the mobile wallet Billetera Personal which is mainly used for money transfer, commercial payment and bill payment. It has provided services for more than one basic account.
CHAPTER 4 MAJOR EXPERIENCE IN DEVELOPMENT OF RURAL FINANCE IN CHINA AND ABROAD

This section primarily discusses the fundamental content, which is the beneficial experiences of applying digitization to the rural finance industry in other countries. It mainly introduces valuable experiences from various countries such as Brazil, the United States, Japan, and others. Finally, after a thorough analysis and summarization, the implementation of group guarantee and other related effective models can be used to control risks, maintain market-oriented operating mechanisms, adopt cutting-edge technologies, and thereby significantly reduce costs. This is aimed at establishing a more systematic model at the level of management incentives and absorbing beneficial and enriching experiences from abroad.
4.1 Major Experience of Brazil in Development of Rural Finance

With respect to the development of digital finance services, Brazil is mainly characterized in its new mode that no point-to-point bank outlet is set up. This is a brand-new development concept. How to understand this mode? The mode will be adopted for areas with scattered agency stores to provide business services for areas not covered by bank outlets or areas difficult in accessing to relevant services. This measure had been promoted and implemented extensively since the year of 2000. Up to now, great achievement has been made with this effort. The mode is still under implementation, and supporting services for the mode are also improved, which pushes forwarding the development of the industry.
(I) Operation mode of banking business without bank outlet

With respect to banking business without bank outlet, financial services will be provided for specific groups by retail agency stores together with financial institutions relevant to banks. The whole process of the business will be completed by the retail agency while banks, as the last link of the business process, are mainly responsible for providing sufficient fund. In this process, Caixa Aqui is the bank to provide fund for target clients through the agency. For accessing relevant business services of agency outlets of Caixa and relevant departments or institutions, clients only have to provide certification documents like generation II ID cards and residence certificates for the whole process from the application to the use of such services.

Being popular in Brazil, this new mode is promoted quickly. As of 2007, many subjects of rural finance have been benefited from the mode. Brazil has promulgated relevant laws and regulations for strengthening the supervision on the mode, which facilitates the evasion of risks in the daily operation of the mode. For instance, relevant law specifies that the central bank may exam the whole process of the bank in retailer selection. If any problem is found in the process, the bank will be held liable for the consequence. According to relevant laws, the central bank also has the right to inspect detailed records of transactions of institutions under the supervision of the bank. To a great extent, this is helpful for eliminating or dissolving relevant difficulties and risks in the early period.
(II) Features of banking business without outlet

Banking business without outlet are characterized as follows: With the business, needs of target clients will be judged and catered for precisely through credit cards and e-banks installed on mobile phones. In this way, clients may complete the transaction without having to go outside or being restricted by the distance. The business may also allow third parties to provide financial services as agencies. For example, small retailers and many other subjects may also entrust relevant professional agencies to handle business on their behalf when target clients are unable to handle business on site, which is a problem difficult to solve in the past.

In addition to the foresaid functional services, clients may also have access to regular services like deposit and withdrawal. Payment-related business relevant to payment is the central task and function of the business mode. Of course, basic functions like deposit business is included. However, other functions are seldom reflected in the mode. According to the in-depth analysis of

relevant features, the mode has great potential in the development. To a great extent, the mode will make up deficiencies of and support existing bank outlets and cut down the operation cost of the bank outlets.
4.2 Major Experience of the USA in Development of Rural Finance

The USA is outstanding in the development of ITFIN industry. Currently, a complete set of ITFIN market has been formed in rural area of the USA. The success of the USA in this aspect is an example for worldwide financial organizations and institutions to learn. Rural bank is the representative in the ITFIN market of the USA, and provides loan, insurance, transfer and deposit services for the impoverished group in rural areas, which are target clients of rural banks.
(I) Operation mode

Rural banks of the USA function in this way: With a prerequisite that the loan amount is safe and sustainable, loan will be granted to a group and group members will monitor each other and reallocate the resources, thus benefiting the whole group. Specifically, five or more impoverished persons may form a group voluntarily; the group members have equivalent economic conditions; the bank will exam credit of potential group members; if the potential group members passing the examination of the bank may be admitted to the interest group and have access to loan. The period of the examination is one week. Group members do not have to provide guarantee or collateral. The process is as follows: Loan will be granted to two impoverished persons in the group. If they are able to repay the loan within specified time limit, loan will be granted to other members of the group. This is helpful for group members to monitor each other. The maturity period of the loan is one year. Borrowers may repay the loan in installments and need to repay one percentage point of the loan in each month and pay off the principal in a year. The process will be maintained to cast off poverty of the impoverished group.
(II) Major features

One of features of rural banks of the USA is that loan interest rates are differentiated based on the purpose of the loan. Loans used for different purpose are relevant to different interest rate. Moreover, no interest will accrued for loan for especially poverty-stricken rural group. The fact shows that most farmers will choose petty loan with low interest rate. The second feature is that high deposit interest rate mechanism is promoted by rural banks of the USA. The rural banks use this measure to attract deposit and strength the lending ability, thus facilitating sustainable development of rural banks.

In addition, rural banks of the USA is distinctively featured in their compulsive deposit policies. As a prerequisite for obtaining loan, a borrower needs to deposit certain amount in the bank within the time limit specified by the bank. The deposit will be considered as the fund for forming a group. Meanwhile, the bank will offer relatively high interest rate for the deposit. After the review of the rural bank, the borrower may obtain loan from the bank on condition that five percent of the raised money should be deposited into the group fund. According to relevant investigation, up to half of the capital of rural banks of the USA is sourced from borrowers’ deposit in commercial banks. Therefore, compulsive deposit has great function on rural banks of the USA as it reduces the number of borrowers, relieves the problem of the interest rate for the repayment and lowers the risk faced by rural banks of the USA.
4.3 Major Experience of Japan in Development of Rural Finance

BRI is one of the largest state-owned commercial financial institutions in the world to provide financial services for rural areas while Japan has the largest client group for the bank. BRI provides financial services for most rural families and carries out other businesses smoothly in Japan.

ITFIN mainly caters for families with low family economic income and economic difficulties. As it is expensive to provide financial services for families with economic difficulties and it is often hard

for banks providing such services for such targets to recover the capital, the subsidiary of the government and the country becomes the only support for relevant banks. Consequently, many banking institutions are not willing to provide financial services and always try to avoid the financial services for those rural families for preventing capital loss. However, Japan solve this problem in a good way: Internet-based petty loan mode and financial services for impoverished families are combined, improved and innovated into a status that the provision of such financial services has no adverse impact on banks. In the end, BRI functions smoothly and makes great achievement in the development of financial services in Japan.
(1) Operation mode of BRI

The supreme banking department in Japan is BRI Credit Department. Established in 1895, BRI has a history of over 100 years. It is one of major state-owned commercial banks of Indonesia. Business of BRI is mainly divided into three parts: commercial finance and petty loan finance. Petty credit functioning in commercial principles is the core business of rural bank system of BRI while ordinary agriculture-related loan developed from policy-based subsidiary loan is the major business of the system.

BRI has institutions of three levels: branch, sub-branch and rural bank. Rural bank falls in the bottom layer of the operation mode of BRI. BRI Credit Department is the dominant department which directs, controls and makes decisions for the credit business. In the operation mode of BRI, BRI Credit Department formulates credit business policies and manages departments of lower levels; each department needs to monitor and manage its subsidiary department and audit the calculation work. Up to now, BRI has set up 324 sub-branches in cities of Japan. These sub-branches are distributed in major cities mainly for carrying out credit business targeting individuals or collectives, monitoring and reviewing credit business of rural banks, and making decisions on the upper limit of loan amount together with rural banks. Rural banks of all levels must bear the expense accrued in the process of credit business. According to statistics, the number of rural banks in Japan has been up to 4046 and keeps going up. These rural banks are distributed in places of Japan under the decision based on BRI mode.

As the core of internet petty loan, rural banks undertake the work of independent financial accounting and examination. Generally, rural banks are set up in urban areas and towns or places near to markets. To reduce operation cost and expenditure, rural banks would recruit employees in local places. This will simplify expenditure and internal structure of banks, reduce expenditure of banks, enhance the communication of banks with local residents, reduce local resources consumed by human resources of banks, and relieve moral risk faced by banks. In addition to the reduction of human resource cost, recruiting personnel in local places will make loan audit simple and convenient, cut down loan service expense, help to evade the risk arising from inaccurate information, and strengthen comprehensive power and competitiveness of the banks.
(II) Major features

Banks under BRI operation mode will offer credit services which allows for installments, which relieve the economic difficulties of relevant families. Loan for operation has a period of maturity of about 24 month, loan for investment has a period of maturity of about 36 months, and individuals or units only have the repay the loan on monthly basis. Borrowers may also repay the loan in lump sum. Generally, the period of repayment may be extended for about 9 months. To prevent borrowers from failing in timely repayment because of shortage in fund or for other reasons, banks will deduct monthly 10% of the interest from the loan to help borrowers to repay the loan. The method of repayment with both interest and principal is adopted by the banks based on the credit risk theory and cash flow theory. In this way, the balance between working capital and financial revenue and expenditure of the banks will be maintained; and the risk faced by the banks in the provision of loan services will be also reduced.

Before releasing the loan, the bank will exam the borrower to determine whether the borrower is able to repay the loan on schedule. After the borrowed is deemed capable of repayment on schedule, the loan will then be approved and released. Moreover, staff of the bank should learn about status of the borrower periodically to lower the risk in credit services.
4.4 Experiences of
Western
Countries and Major
Enlightenment
4.4.1 Control Risk by the Adoption of Group Joint Guarantee Mode

Group joint guarantee mode has substitute the traditional relation between the borrower and the lender and the relation between financial unit and the borrower. To a certain extent, the mode lowers the credit risk caused by asymmetric information and also helps individuals or collectives with economic difficulties. Joint guarantee mode also reduces the risk in credit finance. For example, under the joint guarantee mode adopted in the USA, individuals or collectives will obtain loan under the five-group joint guarantee mode, which effectively reduces the risk in loan activities and maintains the repayment rate of over 98%.
4.4.2 Stick to Marketized Operation Mechanism

Rural finance market will be developed stably only when rural finance follows the law of market. Government’s assistance and support for rural finance is limited. Therefore, rural finance needs to be developed in a marketized and commercial mode. Digital finance cost should be covered via interest rate pricing based on market condition. Sufficient capital sources and healthy development of financial enterprises will be maintained only when Digital finance functions through the market. Major targets of credit business in of rural finance are individuals or families with economic difficulties in rural areas. The target group is characterized in the low amount of loan and extensive distribution, which greatly pushes up the labor cost and expenditure of loan activities in daily work. This is also the cause of the high interest rate. Only high interest rate may sustain the daily expenditure and maintain normal operation of the financial institution. Interest rate of rural banks of the USA is often higher than other banks, which is a key factor for the success of those rural banks. According to statistical data, annual interest rate of rural banks of the USA is about 20% while that of Japan even reaches up to 40%. High annual interest rate is the only way to maintain normal operation, balance of revenue and expenditure and development of financial institutions,
4.4.2 Reduce Cost through Advanced Technologies

Banking business without outlets may be promoted not only in metropolises but also in rural areas. A bank may invest certain amount of fund in the rural area to establish relevant business, complete facilities, reduce the expense for setting up business without outlets, and cut down unnecessary expenditure. We can see from the case of Brazil that the cost of setting up POS machine in areas without outlets is less than USD 3000, and the monthly cost is only USD 160. In contrast, the construction of a physical bank operation premise will cost USD 400,000 and the expense for personnel training and equipment is also high. Therefore, setting up agencies without outlets is much cheaper than the construction of physical operation premise. With this aspect, China may also set up agencies without outlets, reduce operation costs and improve operation efficiency of banks by borrowing the mode of Brazil.
4.4.2 Establish Perfect Management System and Incentive Mechanism

(1) Establish a complete regulation mechanism. Different aspects should be considered for designing a perfect management system for rural areas. The system should be encouraging and prudent, and smooth the way for sustainable healthy development of rural ITFIN and provide a healthy external environment for the development. Development of rural financial industries in countries and regions shows that financial monitoring mechanisms in many regions are complete and a homocentric square standard is set up for rural ITFIN institutions. Therefore, promotion of healthy development of rural ITFIN lies in the establishment and implementation of monitoring
system, making up of deficiencies in rural financial market, and the formulation and implementation of complete systems.

(2) Strengthen human resource construction. In modern society, talent has become an increasingly important key drive for the development of enterprises. So is the case of rural financial market. Long-term healthy development relies on talent introduction and cultivation. As development of rural financial industry has its own features and the market in rural areas is highly potential, it is necessary to consider not only professional knowledge and skills but also market response ability of interviewees in the recruitment of financial workers. There are many problems to be solved in the process of development of financial industry in China: Financial rights and interest of people in many rural areas are not guaranteed; many financial institutions attach little importance to rural market, have little service awareness for rural market, or even develop rural financial market as a business simply for economic benefit other than paying attention to the catering of needs of the rural market and the provision of more convenient financial services for rural market. The most important task for financial institutions is to solve the loan problem of rural areas, lower loan interest rate, promote the development of rural financial industry and implement policies of the government for supporting rural financial market. All the work needs to be down by a number of professional talents who are familiar with the development of rural financial industry and devote to the implementation of policies of relevant departments for supporting the development of rural financial industry and help farmers to solve the most urgent problems. Talents serving the rural areas should be earnest and responsible and have the awareness of serving farmers and passion for joining financial construction of rural areas. Therefore, the work is demanding. Talents should be both professional and experienced.

(3) Perfect reward and punishment mechanism. It is very important for financial workers to strengthen the shift in concept, form the awareness of serving farmers, enhance professional knowledge and skills, understand documents issued by relevant departments in connection with the development of rural financial industry, work with a good attitude and facilitate the development of rural financial industry. It is necessary to care about requirements of employees, cater needs of staff and cultivate employees’ sense of belonging. Employees who are excellent in performance should be rewarded, physically or mentally, in accordance with relevant rules so as to encourage employees to work hard. Meanwhile, it is also necessary to make career planning for employees and define the route for promotion to incite employees to devote to work with a good attitude, improve professional quality, update knowledge and skills, strengthen the awareness of serving rural areas and farmers, work positively and earnestly for the construction of rural finance and promote the stable development of rural financial industry.
CHAPTER 5 CURRENT DEVELOPMENT SITUATION OF RURAL FINANCE IN CHINA
5.1 Current Situation of Development of Rural Finance in China
5.1.1 Moderate-scale Operation Induces Demand for Rural Finance

Rural labor has undergone significant migration, and new agricultural operators continue to emerge, with land transfer and operational scale development becoming an inevitable trend. According to statistics from the Ministry of Agriculture, as of the latest publicly available data in 2020, the total area of contracted arable land transferred reached 530 million mu, exceeding one-third of the total area of contracted arable land. In some eastern coastal areas, the transfer ratio has already exceeded one-half. The number of large-scale farming households with operational arable land exceeding 50 mu has exceeded 3.5 million, covering an area of over 350 million mu. The number of new entities such as family farms, farmer cooperatives, and leading agricultural industrial enterprises has exceeded 2.7 million. As of 2020, there were 2.22 million registered farmer cooperatives nationwide, and the number of farmers joining these cooperatives accounted for nearly 50% of the total number of farmers in the country.
(I) “Three-right” split policy for land to guide moderate-scale development of agriculture

In the early stage of reform and opening, “two-power” split policy incited the activeness of rural residents. Agricultural productivity is low and food is the most pressing matter at the time. With a prerequisite that rural land is owned collectively, the right of land management was contracted to rural residents, and the household contract responsibility system with remuneration linked to output was implemented.

In 2008, the process of confirming rural contracted management rights was initiated, and through the "confirmation of rights" and the issuance of land certificates, the actual rights of land contracting were determined. Following the principles of clear ownership, complete rights, smooth transfer, and strict protection in the property rights system, the central government then deployed the registration and certification of rural collective land ownership and rural residents' contracted management rights. In 2013, the Central Committee issued its first directive, outlining the comprehensive registration and confirmation of rural land rights. In 2016, the pilot program was expanded to include 22 provinces. As of October 2016, 2,545 counties (cities, districts), 29,000 townships, and 492,000 villages had carried out this process, covering a total area of 750 million mu, which is nearly 60% of the total area of family-contracted arable land.

With the advancement of urbanization, a significant number of rural laborers have migrated to urban areas for employment in the secondary and tertiary industries. Many rural residents have transferred their land to others for management. To meet the trend of developing modern agriculture and the need for rural residents to retain contracted land rights while willing to transfer management rights, the "Opinions on Improving the Separation of Ownership, Contract, and Management Rights of Rural Land" was introduced. This policy currently implements the parallel system of collective land ownership, rural residents' contracted land rights, and land management rights, known as the "separation of the three rights." This system upholds collective land ownership, stabilizes the contracted land rights of rural residents, and revitalizes land management rights. It lays the institutional foundation for guiding the orderly transfer of land management rights, promoting moderately scaled agriculture, and driving the development of modern agriculture.

(II) Increasingly more rural population flows to urban areas. Reduction in farm workers induces the need for scale production.

China has made further progress in urbanization and proportion of rural population goes down gradually, which requires for the improvement in per capita output of rural population. As a result, it is a trend to develop scale and modernized agriculture.
Figure 5.1: Proportion of Urban Population and Rural Population to Total Population

Compared with traditional agricultural farming, moderate-scale operation is featured as follows: Firstly, farm area is often large and often exceeds 50 mu. Secondly, more modern agricultural machineries and facilities, such as warm house, greenhouse, large-area irrigation facility, sewing

machine and harvesters, are needed. Thirdly, the input of labor force is shifted from relying on self-owned capital and small amount of loan from other persons to depending equally on financing and self-owned capital. With the expansion of operation scale, credit from the external will occupy more share. Therefore, moderate-scale operation will induce the demand for financing.
(III) Currently, only a small proportion of loan flows to agriculture, rural areas and farmers. Rural financial services should be enhanced.

As of the end of 2015, balance of agriculture-related loan of financial institutions in the banking industry was RMB 26.4 trillion while total loan balance was RMB 94 trillion. Agriculture-related loan balance accounted for nearly 30% of total loan balance. However, owing to the generalized statistical caliber and vague statistical standards of indicators of agriculture-related loan, the data is not able to reflect the degree of support from financial institutions of the banking industry for rural areas. According to the
Special Statistical System of Agriculture-Related Loan

jointly issued by the People’s Bank of China and China Banking Regulatory Commission (CBRC), agriculture-related loan may be classified into three types: loan for rural residents, loan for rural enterprises and organizations, and agriculture-related loan for urban enterprises and organization; the “areas except for municipal administrative regions up to prefecture level and towns set up with the approval of the municipal government” are defined as “rural areas”; loan for enterprises and organization registered in the “rural area” may be classified as “loan for rural enterprises and organizations”. To a certain extent, the rule will lead to virtual increase in agriculture-related loan. For example, the central bank classifies the “loan for real estate industry and building industry of county area” as agriculture-related loan in
China Rural Financial Service Report (2014)
. According to the estimation, loan for agriculture, rural area and farmer accounts for less than 10% of total loan, and the per capital loan is much lower than the national average level.
5.1.2 Rural Land Reform Creates New Market for Agricultural Financial Service

In recent years, China has been pursuing a path of rural financial innovation that extends the urban financial system into rural areas. As of the end of the second quarter of 2023, the balance of loans related to agriculture in financial institutions amounted to 54.6 trillion yuan, with rural loans in China reaching 45.6 trillion yuan, both of which exhibited a year-on-year growth rate of approximately 16%. However, despite these significant figures, they still cannot fully meet the growing demand for agricultural financial services in the face of the "new trends" and "new normal" characterized by large-scale, intensive, specialized, and modernized agricultural development in China. Moreover, due to the underdeveloped economy and extreme income instability among those engaged in agriculture, coupled with deficiencies in the traditional agricultural financial service system, it has had a negative impact on the enthusiasm of enterprises to engage in agricultural finance. It also limits the opportunities for those in the agricultural sector to access financial funds and services. Therefore, there is a need for the expansion and innovation of the existing agricultural financial service system to address these challenges and better serve the evolving needs of the agricultural sector.
According to
Several Opinions on Fully Deepening Rural Reform and Accelerating Agricultural Modernization

(No.1 Document issued by the State Council in 2014), “land right confirmation should be accelerated and transfer of production resources to scale operation subjects should be promoted through land circulation under the free will be rural residents; moderate-scale agricultural operation and cultivation of emerging operation subjects like rural residents and agricultural cooperatives should be encouraged”. The enhancement of scale and intensive operation pushes up the agricultural industrialization level, increases the demand of agricultural workers for operation fund (such as increase in subsidiary for purchasing agricultural machineries with the improvement of agricultural mechanization level), and leads to constant growth of fund for agricultural technology development. It is particularly worth noting that policies published since 2014 have endowed the power and function of capital to land contracting right and management right, pilot

mortgage loan with land contracting right and management right as collateral and pilot land trust in the northeast China and Anhui Province have been conducted, which creates a brand-new market for agricultural financial services. Capitalization of land contracting right and management right solve the problem that financial support for rural areas is not matched with current situation of rural scale operation due to lack of mortgage guarantee. According to the estimation of experts of the Ministry of Agriculture, the scale of loan for land circulation will be up to RMB 1 trillion.
5.1.3 Policies Issued to Encourage Development of Rural Finance
According to the planning specified in the “No.1 Central Document”, the CBRC issued the
Notice on Working Hard to Provide Rural Financial Services in 2015

on March 3, 2015, requiring financial institutions in the banking industry to stabilize, strengthen, and execute existing policies on rural financial services, follow the principle of maintenance, reduction, support and control, increase the credit for “agriculture, rural area and farmer” and expand the issue scale of special financial bonds for “agriculture, rural area and farmer” based on the effective increase in loan amount to push the agriculture-related loan growth rate up to level which is higher than the average growth rate of total loan in 2015. Moreover, it is required to improve rural financial service capability and level by enriching subjects engaged in rural finance and developing rural inclusive finance. With respect to financial innovation, the Notice points out as follows: “Guide financial institutions in approved areas to participate into the pilot project of mortgage loan with rural land contracting right and management right and farmer’s house and property right as collateral; expand the scale of mortgage loan with forest ownership as collateral; encourage financial institutions in the banking industry to draw up agricultural credit plan, delegate loan approval power, simplify business decision-making process, innovate service mode and strengthen service function.”

5.2 China’s Dilemma
in Development of Rural Finance
5.2.1 Vulnerable Foundation for Rural Financial Services

Objectively, agricultural economy and rural economy is vulnerable in the development process, which inevitably leads to the vulnerability of the development of rural finance. The feature is mainly mirrored by the relatively high risk and unstable revenue. In current stage, infrastructure or fundamental conditions of rural financial services in China are relatively weak, which is mainly reflected in following aspects:
(1) Small number of financial institutions relevant to agriculture.

In the past few years, the central government has been attaching increasingly more importance to the development of agricultural economy. In the process of development of rural finance, the central government enhances the construction of rural financial service system. Policy-based financial

institutions, commercial and cooperating financial institutions start to set up outlets, develop rural business and products and leverage the function of rural finance in promoting the development of rural economy. Table 5.3 shows information about China’s financial institutions relevant to agriculture in 2015:
Table 5.3: Name and Other Information about China’s Financial Institutions Relevant to Agriculture in 2020
Institution Name
Number of Institutions
Number of Operation Outlets
Number of Workers in the Industry
Rural credit cooperatives
Rural commercial bank
Rural cooperative bank
Village and town bank
Loan company
Rural mutual financing cooperative
Source: China Banking Regulatory Commission

Table 5.3 shows that the number of financial institution relevant to agriculture in China takes up a great proportion. However, proportions of operation outlets and workers in the industry are relatively low. From the strategic perspective that China attaches increasingly more importance to “agriculture, rural area and farmer” and accelerates the urbanization, number of existing financial institutions relevant to agriculture is much less than the number needed. Shortage of rural financial institutions further worsen the dilemma that rural fundamental financial service is unable to cater the need of rural residents, and the inconvenience in financial services also makes the development of rural finance more difficult.
(2) Weak foundation of rural financial services

In the process of long-term development, rural area has weak foundation for rural financial services due to the relatively poor development foundation and natural environment, incomplete policy-based supporting system for the development of rural finance and imperfect risk system construction. The situation is reflected as follows: Postal Savings Bank of China (PSBC) have set up service outlets in rural areas in China. However, the loan capability of PSBC is limited. Commercial financial institutions (such as the Agricultural Bank of China and Industrial and Commercial Bank of China) which entered in the industry in the beginning of the development of rural finance have started to withdraw from the market of the industry. Rural credit cooperative is the major force for the development of rural finance. However, there are many problems in the development of rural credit cooperative, such as weak foundation, large amount of debt and poor risk-preventing ability. To a great extent, the problems affect the function of rural credit cooperative in promoting the development of rural finance. In addition, some rural credit cooperatives excellent in operation performance accept the reform actively in the tide of marketization reform and tend to be separated from agriculture. In these years, a number of emerging rural financial institutions are developed with the original intention of making up the vacancy of development of rural finance. However, the short development time and problematic development strategic and product positioning make the number of outlets and overall development scale unsatisfactory. There are also objective restrictions in regional factor. Consequently, these institutions did not make any

substantial change to the structure of rural finance. Rural residents and small and medium-sized enterprises are faced with a narrow financing channel and high financing cost owing to the development situation of rural finance with weak service foundation. Consequently, these groups are forced to choose privately-owned financial institutions, which greatly reduces the credit availability.
5.2.2 Deficiency in Information Channel of Rural Financial Market

Rural areas are often relatively backward in economic development, scattered in distribution, and lagging behind in system binding construction and information channel construction, which tends to cause the situation of information asymmetry. Information asymmetry is common in the development of rural financial development, which directly makes it difficult for rural financial institutions and financial institution relevant to agriculture to truly understand effective demand of rural residents and agricultural enterprises and analyze their demand for loan through mastering the credit status and enterprise operation condition of these groups. Consequently, the credit threshold is raised up, which affects the rationality of credit allocation. In addition, loan flows in various ways and loan capital purposes vary from one another, thus making it more difficult for relevant departments of rural financial institutions in monitoring, increasing the monitoring cost and, to certain extent, pushing up the probability of moral risk of the credit market. Therefore, rural financial institutions have to be always prudent. Loan screening effect caused by information asymmetry will affect market clearing and credit allocation. In this circumstance, low-income group will bear not only the pain of low-value consumption caused by lack of assets but also the tiredness caused by low income. As a result, some rural residents in rural areas will be rejected from accessing credit services from financial institutions, which is unfavorable for healthy development of rural finance. In the process of rural finance system reform in these years, China has strengthened the efforts in making up the deficiency in information channels, such as through the setting up of village and town banks. However, the effect of the efforts needs to be observed in the future.
5.2.3 Simple Structure of Rural Financial Product
s

Rural economy witnesses constant changes with the unceasing development of social economy in China. The deepening and acceleration of rural reform is accompanied by favorable achievement in agricultural economy with respect to diversified development and improvement of technology content of production. The development of rural finance shows that the changes directly increase effective demand for the development of rural finance. In current stage, Chinese government is active in the construction of rural financial system and also issues preferential policies to encourage rural financial institutions to innovate relevant financial products and services for matching increasing effective demand in rural areas. Measures of the government are effective to certain extent. However, the structure of rural financial products is still simple and unable to form a rural product system with rich content in current stage. Compared with ordinary financial products, the structure of rural financial products is simple in three aspects:
Table 5.4: Simple Structure of Rural Financial Products
Rural Financial Products
Ordinary Financial Products
Time
Provide credit services based on agricultural production seasons
Often not restricted by time
Target
Agricultural production materials such as farm chemicals, chemical fertilizer and seed
Financial products cover various industries
Method
Often do statistical work with forms based on requirements of meetings
Conduct statistical work and analysis with modernized methods
Table 5.4 shows that the structure of rural financial products is simple. The purpose of the

development of rural finance is to push forward the reform and development of rural economy and better serve rural economy. Therefore, time, target and other factors need to correspond to features of agricultural economy during the design of rural financial products. However, under the strategic background of reform and innovation, supply-side structural reform and urbanization construction, agricultural production is not the only way for the development of agricultural economy. Instead, there has been new demand for rural financial products, such as novel agricultural product future. The simple structure of rural financial products in current stage is hard to adapt to the changing development situation.
5.2.4 “Difficulties” of Borrower and Lender in Rural Finance

Rural financial institutions, rural residents and small and medium-sized enterprises are subjects of rural financial activities. Major content of rural financial activities is loan. However, the fact in China in current stage is that “rural residents are confronted with difficulty in obtaining loan while rural financial institutions are faced with difficulty in lending”, which greatly affects the effect of function of rural financial institutions and impedes the development of rural economy.
(I) Difficulty of rural residents in obtaining loan
(1) Difficulty of ordinary rural residents in obtaining loan

For instance, target borrowers of rural credit cooperatives are ordinary rural residents which are characterized in large number, scattered distribution and relatively small farming scale. However, system construction and process optimization of rural credit cooperative is not perfect, and the loan process is relatively complicated. Therefore, it is hard for an ordinary rural resident to receive an affirmative reply in a timely manner after the loan application is submitted to a rural credit cooperative. In this circumstance, ordinary rural residents often turn to their relatives and friends for borrowing money or just reduce the input intentionally in the process of farming, which will form a vicious circle. In spite of the small scale of individual ordinary rural resident, total number of ordinary rural residents is huge. If the situation remains unchanged, agricultural production will be greatly affected, which will not only lead to low agricultural production efficiency but also affect the agricultural development strategy of China.
(2) Difficulty of large agricultural operators in obtaining loan

In this paper, large agricultural operator means a professional operator engaged in certain agricultural sector and operating the business in certain scale. Different from ordinary rural residents, large agricultural operators carry out the production in the way of enterprise operation and make efforts to realize modernized operation and management in the process of operation. In this process, however, they will encounter the most serious and realistic problem that they are short of fund. In the beginning, the government assists large agricultural operators in expanding the production and planting scale through preferential policies and other forms in order to stimulate activeness of these large agricultural operators in the development. However, these policies are not implemented constantly. The problem of fund shortage remains unsolved. The call for “lending” by rural financial institutions is often answered in the beginning and neglected in the end. The difficulty of large agricultural operators in obtaining loan is not eliminated substantially.
(3) Difficulty of farmers’ professional cooperatives in obtaining loan

Ordinary rural residents and large agricultural operators are major members of farmers’ professional cooperatives. The difference of the cooperatives from ordinary rural residents and large agricultural operators is that the former is more professional. Therefore, the cooperatives are still faced with the difficulty in obtaining loan. Strictly speaking, farmers’ professional cooperatives are set up with a good intention. However, the cooperatives are incapable in operation, which also affects the healthy development of farmers’ production and operation organizations. Table 5.5 mainly shows the agriculture-related loans in China from 2011 to 2015.
Table 5.5: Agriculture-Related Loan in China from 2011 to 2022 (Unit: RMB 100 Million)
Period
Loan for Rural residents
Balance of All Loans

Proportion to All Loans

As shown in Table 5.5, although the balance of loan for rural residents was increasing and the proportion of the loan for rural residents to total loan also went by year by year during the period from 2011 to 2022, absolute value and proportion of the balance were still relatively low.
(II) Difficulty of financial institutions in releasing loan

In current stage, rural financial institutions or financial institutions relevant to agriculture may be classified into two types: The first type is policy-based and commercial financial institutions including Chinese-invested large-scale banks and Chinese-invested national medium-scale banks. The second type is cooperating or privately-owned financial organizations. These rural financial institutions or financial institutions need to follow operation and management concepts of modern enterprises and the law of market economy operation and define strict loan application and approval process. After receiving a loan application, most of such financial institutions would request the applicant to submit collateral with clear property right and reasonable value. However, many ordinary rural residents and large agricultural operators are unable to meet the requirement. Consequently, impact of these external factors leads to the difficulty of rural financial institutions in releasing loan, and the function of rural financial institutions can not be fully leveraged.
Table 5.6: Agriculture-Related Loan of China from 2017 to 2022 (Unit: RMB 100 Million)
Period
Loan for Rural residents
Balance of All Loans
Balance of the Period
Proportion to All Loans

According to the information about agriculture-related loan released by financial institutions, the proportion of full-caliber balance of agriculture-related loan to total loans tended to go up year by year, but the absolute value and proportion of the balance were not high during the period from 2017 to 2022
5.2.5 Deviation
in Credit Flow Direction of Rural Financial Institutions
The central government requires to “enhance the duties of financial institutions for serving
‘agriculture, rural area and farmer’

”, “strengthen the service ability of commercial finance for ‘agriculture, rural area and farmer’ and county-level small and micro enterprises, enhance business power delegation to county-level branch institutions, increase the deposit-loan rate and the proportion of agriculture-related loan, and include the offer of agricultural credit into policy-oriented effect evaluation and comprehensive assessment system”. Requirements of the central government show that rural financial institutions and financial institutions relevant to agriculture need to support the development of “agriculture, rural area and farmer” by formulating support policies and designing specific financial products to respond to requirements of the government. The core task of rural financial institutions and financial institutions relevant to agriculture is to solve fund problems encountered in the process of development of “agriculture, rural area and farmer” by expanding the scale of agriculture-related loan and increasing the proportion of agriculture-related loan through the leverage of their functions, thus pushing forward the healthy development of “agriculture, rural area and farmer” in China. However, the situation is not attached with enough importance, resulting in the deviation in credit flow direction of rural financial institutions.
(I) Attach great importance to non-agricultural undertaking and little importance to “agriculture, rural area and farmer”

In the market economic environment, many rural financial institutions will not only actively evade the risk in credit but also evade business in connection with “agriculture, rural area and farmer” with relatively high risk in their setting of credit flow direction to realize the commercial operation benefit. The conduct of these rural financial institutions leads to serious deviation in credit flow direction. Some rural financial institutions which mainly engaged in business supporting “agriculture, rural area and farmer” deviates from the orientation of main business to chase commercial benefit in the marketized environment. For example, a rural credit cooperative association in a town which has the major function of providing fund and policy-based support for the development of “agriculture, rural area and farmer” in the area planned to release loan of RMB 310 million in the year. However, 48% of the loan was released to industrial enterprises. The proportion indicates that rural financial institutions attach great importance to non-agricultural undertaking and little importance to “agriculture, rural area and farmer” and deviate seriously from the operation objective of the institutions. Owing to the attraction of more commercial benefit, the proportion may keep going up in the future, which seriously deviates from the aim of “rural credit cooperatives”, as mentioned by the central government, to “stick to serving farmers, agriculture and rural economy”. The situation calls for the attention of the whole society. Generally, agriculture-related loan includes loan for agriculture, forestry industry and fishing industry, loan for circulation of agricultural materials and farm and sideline products, loan for rural infrastructure construction, loan for agricultural product processing and so on, as shown in Table 5.7.
Table 5.7: Uses of Agriculture-Related Loan of China in 2020
Use
Proportion
Loan for agriculture, forestry industry and fishing industry,
Loan for circulation of agricultural materials and farm and sideline products
Loan for rural infrastructure construction
Loan for agricultural product processing
Loan for agricultural production materials manufacturing
Loan for fundamental construction of farmland
