Source: Statistics and Analysis Department of the People’s Bank of China

Table 5.7 shows that the proportion of agriculture-related loan used for agricultural production, agricultural infrastructure construction, agricultural R&D and other agricultural activities was only 40.8% while the proportion of agriculture-related loan used for other purposes was 59.2%, which directly indicates that use of the loan was deviated.
(II) Unclear important point

Key targets to be supported and assisted by rural financial institutions are not defined in the process of development, which is one of the reasons for the deviation in credit flow direction. Since agricultural economy was developed, production and operation of farmers have witnessed great changes. For example, a household with a farm area of 5 mu is a rural resident while a household with a farm area of 500 mu is also listed as a rural resident. The two rural residents are not only different in farming scale. Most importantly, production and operation modes and the demand for fund are substantially different. Under the background of development of agricultural economy with great efforts, rural resident of the latter should be considered as key target to support and assist in the development of rural finance. As this is a trend of development, the country should implement agriculture-supporting policies through rural finance instead of adopting existing supporting measure.

To push forward the development of “agriculture, rural area and famer” with rural finance, national and local departments have published a number of policies, rules and opinions which are mainly about the constant strengthening of support for agricultural economic organizations. However, the actual effect of implementation indicates that China has not yet formulated or published relatively clear and feasible implementation measures. In other words, the problem of “lacking keys and being unclear” in the process of formulating policies and measures relevant to rural finance is still unsolved.
5.2.6 Financial Institutions of Multiple Levels not Formed into Agriculture-Supporting Compound Forc
e

In these years, China has been devoted to the construction of a rural financial institution system with complete functions and clear layer and structure and encouraging policy-based banks, commercial banks and new rural financial institutions to join in the construction. In the process of the construction, actual situation of development of rural finance, especially the situation of demand and supply, should be taken into consideration, and more financial institutions, especially rural financial institutions and financial institutions engaged in agriculture-related business, should be

encouraged to join in the efforts. To construct such a multi-layer rural financial system, efforts must be made in two aspects: Firstly, medium-sized and large banks participating into the construction should play a leading role, design financial products and services relevant to agricultural development and establish a perfect service mechanism. Secondly, it is necessary to attract new rural financial institutions to participate into the construction to enhance the competition among financial institutions in the system and drive rural financial institutions to strengthen internal construction, perfect their products and services and improve service efficiency. Therefore, the construction of a multi-layer rural financial institution system is a long-term systematic large project. Only with joint efforts of all parties will the system construction be promoted and will the function of the system in driving the development of agricultural economy be leveraged. Actual situation indicates that the construction of the system is defective in following aspects:
(I) Agricultural insurance institution is not developed sufficiently

Experience of developed countries represented by the USA shows that insurance industry has closely combined with the development of agriculture. The development of insurance products and services targeting agriculture are relatively mature. However, insurance companies targeting agricultural development have not yet developed in China in current stage. In this aspect, there is still a large gap between China and those developed countries. Currently, China has only published policy-based rules on the support from insurance companies for agricultural development. More importantly, the insurance-related supporting fund is provided for rural residents by non-agricultural insurance institutions as agencies. Objectively speaking, natural conditions, climates and other uncertain factors have great impact on agricultural production. Agricultural production and operation are exposed to relatively high risk, which is consistent with the positioning of insurance. Therefore, it is suitable to development agricultural insurance. In the past few years, there has been saying that agricultural insurance companies will be established in China. However, no substantial progress has been made in this aspect. Meanwhile, privately-owned cooperating insurance institutions are not developed maturely and only have limited function. These institutions are unable to play the role of shifting risks confronted by “agriculture, rural area and farm”.
(II) Function of trust investment companies targeting rural areas are not fully leveraged

Nature of agricultural economy decides the relatively low input-output rate of the industry. The development of trust investment institutions is much more backward than the development of relatively lagging agricultural insurance. In current stage, few trust investment institutions can be found in the development of rural economy, which greatly restricts the business expansion by trust investment companies in agricultural sector and makes it hard for trust investment companies to leverage their functions in the process of supporting the development of “agriculture, rural area and farmer”.
(III) Non-governmental mutual financing cooperative grows slowly

Theoretically, the establishment of non-governmental mutual financing cooperative will make up the deficiencies of rural financial services to a certain extent. However, the actual situation is that function of non-governmental mutual financing cooperatives is not leveraged in the development process of rural finance in China. There is a large gap between theory and practice. Generally, if a rural resident or an agricultural enterprise has demand for loan because of fund problems, they will choose to apply for loan from financial institutions in the first place. However, according to internal system, financial institutions are required to exam the loan application received, credit status of the loan applicant, existing loan record, collateral and so on. Moreover, it is a long time to handle the loan application and relevant formalities are also complicated, which is obviously unable to meet the urgent demand of the rural residents or agricultural enterprises for fund. Non-governmental mutual financing cooperatives are advantageous over other financial institutions with respect to loan application, qualification review and formality-handling procedure and able to meet the demand of

rural residents or agricultural enterprises for fund in short time. Therefore, Non-governmental mutual financing cooperatives are “supplementary” to the function of financial institutions. With respect to the slow development of non-governmental mutual financing cooperatives, the country needs to push forward the development of such organizations by policy-based guidance and support. In summary, it is a heavy and time-consuming task to construct a multi-layer financial institution system. All subjects of the system need to participate into the construction. At present, there is still a great gap between current development status and ideal status.
Analysis of Causes for Slow Development of Rural Finance in China
5.3.1 Infrastructure

If we strictly categorize rural financial institutions and agricultural financial institutions, regular institutions providing rural financial services include Agricultural Bank, Postal Savings Bank, Rural Commercial Banks, and others. Typically, they establish business branches in township areas to directly purchase or lease the necessary infrastructure to create financial service platforms. In this context, the ability to successfully establish these platforms, as well as the specific geographic location of these platforms, significantly impacts the degree to which financial institutions can fulfill their functions. It also has a substantial impact on the implementation of national financial policies.

Furthermore, from the earlier discussion regarding the proportion of business branches of agricultural financial institutions, it's evident that there are still many underdeveloped areas in rural China. If the infrastructure for rural financial development is lacking, it can significantly hinder the effectiveness of rural financial services. Firstly, underdeveloped infrastructure for rural financial development can impede the expansion of financial services in rural areas. Typically, rural financial institutions establish branches and service platforms in county or township areas, which increases the distance between rural households and agricultural businesses, making it more difficult for them to access financial products and services. Even today, many rural households still consider the functionality of banks to be limited to savings and currency exchange, which is not conducive to fully utilizing the functions of rural financial institutions. Secondly, underdeveloped infrastructure in rural financial development can lead to information asymmetry. According to standard procedures, rural financial institutions assign professional credit officers to collect information from rural households after receiving loan applications. However, due to operational constraints, the number of information collectors is relatively limited, and the distance between financial institution branches and rural households is typically quite long. This significantly affects the efficiency and quality of the work of credit officers, leading to information asymmetry, which, in turn, negatively impacts business operations. In this scenario, it can unintentionally prolong the loan processing period and result in delayed loans to rural households and agricultural businesses with funding needs. Ultimately, this reduces the attractiveness of rural financial institutions to rural households and agricultural businesses. Additionally, the existence of this "last mile" challenge poses a risk of customer attrition for rural financial institutions.
Market

Actual situation indicates that agriculture is less competitive that industry and service industry with respect to the access to financial services. From the perspective of market supply, many rural financial institutions are aware of that the enterprise profit created by agriculture-related financial products and services is not ideal and thy may be even faced with the risk of loss. Consequently, if rural financial institutions have better choices, they will put rural residents and agricultural enterprises behind when they ranking their choices. Some rural financial institutions may even absorption depot in rural areas and loan to non-agricultural sectors or non-rural areas, which leads to the flowing out of capital which is supposed to use for agricultural development, thus causing unbalance in total amounts. From the perspective of market demand, rural residents and agricultural
enterprises are relatively difficult in accessing financial services as stated above.

With reference to the theory of market supply and demand, if the supply is reduced, it will be more difficult to meet the demand or the cost for meeting the demand will be increased greatly. As for the development of rural finance, reduction of supply makes it harder to meet the demand of rural residents and agricultural enterprises for fund and then push rural residents and agricultural enterprises to skip over regular financial institutions and borrow money from relatives and friends to solve the fund problem. In this aspect, the situation will further reduce the reliance of the development of
“agriculture, rural area and farmer”

on regular financial institutions. If the demand is reduced, the supply will be reduced correspondingly. In the process of development, if rural financial institutions find that less fund is needed for the development of “agriculture, rural area and farmer”, the institutions will reduce the supply correspondingly, which will make it harder for rural residents and agricultural enterprises to access financial products and services. The foresaid situation will form a vicious cycle, and each process will be repeated unceasingly. In the end, benefit of both the supply side and demand side will be affected.
5.3.3 Policies

In current stage, market failure in the process of development of rural finance in China is relatively serious due to impact of many factors. Therefore, function of “the visible hand” should be leveraged in this circumstance, and policy-based tools and measures should be used to solve problems caused by market failure. For example, the country will revise laws and regulations to guarantee that a favorable environment is provided for the development rural finance and request some rural financial institutions to set up service outlets or service platforms in grass-root units to promote the development of rural finance. However, the government, being “the visible hand”, is not always powerful. Failures may be caused by some internal and external factors. Such failures are referred to as the government failures.
(I) “Government failure” in rural finance construction

(1) Usually, outlets and financial service platforms of rural financial institutions are set up in county towns or towns. Generally speaking, there are much more financial institution outlets and service platforms in county towns than in towns. There are many reasons for the phenomena. The most important factor is probably that staffs of these outlets and platforms live in county towns and towns. If the outlets and platforms are established in rural areas, it will be inconvenient for these staffs. The distribution of outlets and platforms leads to long distance from rural financial infrastructure to rural areas, thus causing negative problems like information asymmetry, which is not favorable for healthy development of rural finance.

(2) To push forward the development of rural finance, the country designates a number of institutions mainly engaged in rural financial business, such as Rural Credit Cooperatives, intending to pushing forward the development of rural financial business with these financial institution outlets as carriers. However, many rural financial institutions or agriculture-related financial institutions focus on business development in urban areas, only carry out business targeting large-scale agricultural enterprises and rich rural residents and keep distance with impoverished rural residents and small and medium-sized enterprises due to the existence of market failure. Obviously, these institutions run in the opposite direction against the intention of the country. 
(II) “Government failure” in rural finance regulation

Currently, the regulation of rural finance is still a heavy burden and takes a long time. There are still problems in the regulation work. Firstly, rise in income level of rural areas in current stage creates increasing demand of rural areas for financial management. Flow direction of the large amount of surplus fund has relatively important impact. Currently, regulation on the surplus fund in China is not effective enough, which causes vile events, such as illegal fund-raising, and creates adverse impact on social development. In addition, some petty loan companies are developed in the market

to respond to the imbalance between financial supply and financial demand in rural areas. These petty loan companies are small in scale but seeded and growing in the soil of rural areas. Therefore, they are able to meet financing demand of rural areas. In the process of development, the country also publishes preferential policies to support the development of these petty loan companies. However, as the policies are not open enough in the implementation process and institutions and talents with the power of regulation are all seated in county town, some mass events occur due to ineffective regulation. Generally speaking, government failure is mainly reflected in two aspects: excessive regulation and inadequate regulation.
(1) Excessive regulation

As the supply of and demand for financial products and services in rural areas are unbalanced for long term and the supply is less than demand, many petty loan institutions are developed gradually and increase financial supply in rural areas, thus playing an important role in meeting credit demand of rural residents. However, petty loan institutions are often characterized in the small development scale and low system construction and risk management level. moreover, excessive commercialization of these institutions will result in crisis. To cope with this situation, the country raises up the market admission threshold for these institutions: As the market position of these petty loan institutions is not specified in laws issued before, these petty loan institutions are not eligible for enjoying preferential policies about the development of rural finance which are issued by the country. The People’s Bank of China specifies the business scope of these institutions. For example, these institutions are not allowed to absorb deposit of the public in order to improve the safety level of enterprise operation and lower the probability of risk factors. Some national and regional preferential policies only target financial institutions like the Rural Credit Cooperatives. The petty loan institutions are excluded from the policies, which invisibly leads to unfairness in market competition and tends to cause “monopoly” in some sectors. The situation is harmful to the healthy development of rural finance.
(2) Inadequate regulation

According to laws of China, the CBRC has the authority of regulating financial institutions. In practice, local offices of CBRC focus on the regulation of large-scale and medium-sized banks and delegate power of regulation on rural petty loan institutions to grass-root offices. Due to the busy work on regulation, inadequate number of working personnel and lack of professional talents, the regulation on rural petty loan institutions is ineffective or even not conducted, which causes some serious events. Problems caused by ineffective regulation will cause loss of attractiveness of rural petty loan institutions and also harm the healthy development of rural finance.
Summary

The analysis of reasons for current situation of under-developed rural finance shows that fundamental factors restricting the development of rural finance includes four aspects: Firstly, “small, scattered and remote” natural villages lead to difficulty in rational and concentrated construction of financial infrastructure, thus leading to “poor contact” between the supply side and demand side and causing information asymmetry. Secondly, high service cost leads to difficulty in sustainable supply of rural financial services. Thirdly, long-term inadequate supply in the market creates a vicious cycle which leads to market depression. Fourthly, inadequate power of regulation institutions causes difficulty in regulation.
CHAPTER 6 EMPIRICAL STUDY IN DRIVING FUNCTION OF INTERNET ON DEVELOPMENT OF RURAL FINANCE

In this chapter, empirical study will be conducted to exam the relation between web user coverage rate in rural area and the development level of rural credit, thus exploring, in the macroscopic level, whether the popularization of internet in rural area has any positive effect on the development of rural finance.
6.1 Selection of indicators

The dependent variable in this study is the financial development level in rural areas, which is used to measure the ease or difficulty with which the rural population in a given region obtains financial services at a certain cost. Previous literature often uses actual financial indicators obtained in a specific region as measures, such as the presence or absence of bank loans and the total amount of loans received (He Ren et al., 2012; Lu Yajuan, 2014). Some scholars have used the sum of national budget funds,
Chinese
loans,
Western
capital utilization, and the portion of
Chinese

loans in rural non-agricultural investment funds over the years to measure the financial development level of households at the provincial level (Gao Yuandong and Zhang Weiguo, 2014; Hu Zongyi and Luo Liudan, 2016). Drawing from previous research methods, this study employs the per capita amount of rural loans in various provinces to measure the level of financial development in rural areas.

The independent variable is the extent of Internet penetration in rural areas. Given considerations from previous literature and data availability, the study selects the macro-level measure of internet coverage (referred to as "internet user coverage") among the rural population in each province. Internet user coverage is typically measured as the proportion of rural internet users to the total rural population. A higher proportion indicates a broader internet coverage among rural populations. As there is no available data on rural internet users in national statistics over the years, this study borrows the method commonly used in previous research to estimate the number of rural internet users, which involves calculating the product of the total number of internet users in each province and the proportion of urban and rural internet users.

In terms of control variables, referencing literature on empirical research in rural finance (Gao Yuandong, Wen Tao, Wang Xiaohua, 2013; Hu Yizong and Li Peng, 2013), regions with higher per capita income in rural areas tend to have a higher level of financial services. In terms of financial situations, the financial status of regional governments is a critical factor that may influence the coverage of local credit services. Recognizing the importance of financial expenditure related to agriculture in rural finance, areas with higher financial support for agriculture tend to have more significant investments in rural infrastructure and welfare development. At the same time, the growth rate and structure of the social productivity value within rural areas may also affect the level of financial services in the locality, such as the speed and intensity of credit extension. Based on these considerations, this study includes the following control variables: per capita annual income of rural households, per capita financial expenditure within the region, GDP growth rate, and GDP structure.

Data Processing

This paper focuses on the level of rural financial development and utilizes Chinese People's Bank's agricultural loan data from 2009 to 2016 as the source of the dependent variable. Data from 28 provinces and directly-administered municipalities across the country were selected for the analysis, excluding Beijing, Shanghai, and Tibet, based on data completeness.

The treatment of the independent variable is somewhat complex and involves three indicators for each province: the scale of internet users, the proportion of internet users in rural households, and the total number of rural residents. The data for the scale of internet users and the proportion of internet users in urban and rural areas for each province were obtained from the China Internet Network Information Center (CNNIC) database. The data for the number of rural residents in each province were collected from the CEIC database's statistical yearbooks.

The control variables were sourced from various statistics data available in the CEIC database, including the "China Statistical Yearbook," "China Financial Yearbook," "China Fiscal Yearbook," and "China Rural Statistical Yearbook." These data covered various economic, financial, and social statistics relevant to the provincial level.
The data processing and modeling regression were carried out using the econometrics software Stata MP 14.

Based on data features, the author chooses the fixed effects model, one of the panel data estimation models, in the study. (I) Internet popularization in rural areas

Empirical results show that there is significant positive correlation between the development of rural finance and rural internet popularization degree. After variables including regional economy, finance and society are controlled, the relation between the development of rural finance and rural network coverage rate is tested with the model. According to the result in Table 6.4, there is significant positive correlation between per capita amount of agriculture-related loan of rural resident and rural web user coverage rate, which indicates that higher web user coverage rate is correlated to higher per capita amount of agriculture-related loan of rural resident. In summary, rural areas with higher degree of network popularization are better developed in rural finance. The empirical result is consistent with the hypothesis mentioned above: Internet popularization in rural areas
is positively correlated with

the development of rural finance. The phenomena may be explained in several aspects: Firstly, popularization of internet technology may reduce the transaction cost of rural financial services, such as cost of credit services in obtaining clients, time cost of approval process and subsequent management cost, thus
supporting

the development of rural finance. Secondly, application of internet technology may reduce the change of information asymmetry before and after the transaction. If internet technology is used for rural financial services, information about rural residents which is hard to access to with traditional credit measures may be obtained in extensive angles and scope, thus facilitating the reduction in the chance of information asymmetry, rational screening of credit clients and effective risk control. Thirdly, popularization of internet among rural residents may also change rural residents’ way of choosing credit and will of obtaining financial services and push rural residents to shift the mode of borrowing from individuals or privately-owned institutions to the mode of seeking for more formal and convenient financial services.
(II) Income level of rural residents

Empirical results show that there is significant positive correlation between the development of rural finance and income level of rural residents. In this paper, the author uses per capita annual income of rural resident as an indicator to measure income level of rural residents in rural areas. According to the modeling result, higher per capita annual income of rural resident is correlated to better development of rural finance in local place. High income may stimulate rural residents’ demand for credit, thus
supporting
the development of rural finance.
(III) Level of agriculture-related financial expenditure in local place

Empirical results show that there is significant positive correlation between the development of rural finance and the level of agriculture-related financial expenditure in local place. In the model, higher agriculture-related per capita financial expenditure is correlated to better development of rural finance, which is probably because the agriculture-related per capita financial expenditure is closely relevant to the construction of rural infrastructure. For one thing, improvement of infrastructure will drive physical reduction in transaction cost of financial services. For another, improvement of infrastructure improves life of rural residents, which further pushes up the demand for financial services. Therefore, the development of rural finance is pushed forward generally.
(IV) GDP growth rate

Empirical results show that the development of rural finance is negatively correlated to the GDP growth rate. GDP growth rate in the model refers to that of the province. A province with higher GDP growth rate has lower level of development of rural finance. The empirical result may be

illustrated in this way: A province with higher GDP growth rate may pay less attention to rural areas but input more credit resources into urbanization construction. Consequently, rural finance is not sufficiently developed.
(V) GDP structure

Empirical results show that the development of rural finance is positively correlated to the GDP structure. GDP structure in the model refers to the proportion of GDP of the primary industry to total GDP of the province. Higher proportion of GDP of the primary industry is correlated to better development of rural finance and indicates that agriculture, forestry industry and fishing industry are more important. Therefore, the province concerned may attach more importance to the development of rural finance, and further
supporting
the development.

Meanwhile, development level of rural finance is positively correlated to per capita income of rural resident in the region. Based on this feature, the author will further studies on the function of income level and test the impact of income level on the relation between rural internet popularization and development of rural finance.

Summary
of Empirical Study

Based on the empirical analysis conducted based on fixed effects model of panel data of rural internet popularization degree, credit data of rural financial institution, and data of economy, finance and society in 29 provinces and municipalities in China during the eight years from 2009 to 2016, the author draws a conclusion as follows:

The development level of rural finance is positively correlated with the internet penetration rate among rural residents in various provinces nationwide, which is consistent with the theoretical hypothesis discussed earlier. In other words, the widespread adoption of internet and digital technology in rural areas and among rural households
is a significant factor associated with
the positive development of rural finance. Further research reveals that in regions with higher per capita income levels among rural households, the positive
association
of internet adoption among rural residents on the development of rural finance is even stronger. This phenomenon can be explained primarily from three perspectives.

Firstly, popularization and promotion of digital technology in rural areas and among rural residents is helpful for financial institutions to reduce transaction cost of rural financial service. As rural areas are often remote and lack of information communication and scale effect, the development of rural finance in rural areas often costs more than in urban areas. Internet popularization may reduce the client-obtaining cost for credit services and allow rural residents to obtain first-hand financial services and advice without having to go outside. Meanwhile, internet popularization is also helpful for financial institutions to save time cost for the approval process and subsequent management cost, which effectively reduces the transaction cost of rural financial services, thus pushing forward the development of rural finance.

Secondly, application of digital technology may reduce the chance of information asymmetry before and after financial transaction. Availability and accuracy of information about rural residents is always the difficult point of rural financial services. Internet technology may help financial institutions to open up the black box of rural resident information and obtain, in more angles and more extensive scope, the information which is hard to access to with traditional credit measures, thus reducing the chance of information asymmetry, facilitating financial institutions in screening out credit clients and realizing effective risk control. Generally, internet popularization makes information about rural residents more transparent and reduces the chance of information asymmetry before and after rural financial transactions.
Thirdly, the popularization of internet and digital technology in rural areas and among rural

residents may change rural residents’ way to choose credit services and their will of obtaining financial services, push rural residents to shift the mode of borrowing from individuals or privately-owned institutions to the mode of seeking for more formal and convenient financial services. This will change the subjective demand for the development of rural financial services.

Finally, it is worth noting that beyond the quantitative economic indicators included in our model, the regional heterogeneity in government support policies is also a crucial non-negligible factor. While our model controls for agriculture-related fiscal expenditure, the significant differences in rural finance development across provinces (e.g., the disparity between coastal developed regions and inland regions) may also be partially attributed to unobserved variations in local government efficiency and the intensity of policy implementation. The proactive role of local governments in establishing digital platforms, fostering a credit environment, and promoting digital literacy likely acts as a catalyst that amplifies the positive link between digital technology and rural finance, although these 'soft' policy factors are difficult to quantify explicitly in the regression model.

For instance, developed provinces like Jiangsu have established market-oriented mechanisms such as the 'Provincial Inclusive Finance Risk Compensation Fund' (e.g., 'Su Nong Dai') and integrated financial data platforms, which act as powerful catalysts for digital adoption. In contrast, less developed regions
, such as Xinjiang,

may rely more on traditional direct subsidies and lack such digital infrastructure. These 'soft' policy factors likely amplify the positive link between digital technology and rural finance, even though they are difficult to quantify explicitly in the regression model.

In the study, the author verifies the positive effect of internet and digital popularization in rural areas and among rural residents on the development of rural finance, laying a creditable demonstration foundation for the in-depth study on function of internet technology on the effective development of rural finance.
CHAPTER 7
THE CURRENT STATUS OF THE APPLICATION OF DIGITAL TECHNOLOGY IN RURAL FINANCIAL DEVELOPMENT

Based on the empirical research findings mentioned earlier, this chapter will further analyze how digital technology promotes rural finance development at the practical application level. It will specifically discuss the advantages of these applications, the application models, and the current issues in the application phase.

According to Xie Ping, Zou Chuanwei, and Liu Haier (2014), digital finance can be categorized into six forms, including financial digitization (such as online banks, mobile banking, online securities companies, online insurance companies, online financial trading platforms, and online sales of financial products), mobile payments and third-party payments, digital currencies, credit scoring and online loans based on big data, P2P online loans, and crowdfunding. Each of these different forms of digital finance has unique characteristics and can contribute to the inclusive development of rural finance at various levels.
7.1 The Advantages of the Application of Digital Technology in Rural Financial Development

The development of rural finance needs to overcome the financial exclusion caused by accessibility barriers. Digital finance leverages its advantages primarily in four ways to overcome traditional barriers in rural financial services:
Digital platforms help improve rural financial infrastructure.
Digital finance enhances the accessibility of financial services for rural households.
Digital finance
is a significant factor associated with
reducing the costs associated with rural financial services.
Digital technology aids in enhancing the regulatory capacity of rural finance.
7.1.1 Enhancing Rural Financial Infrastructure

The characteristics of "small, scattered, and remote" natural villages in rural areas have resulted in a fundamental lack of information, which is one of the root causes of the underdevelopment of rural finance. To address this problem, there are primarily two approaches: the establishment of physical infrastructure and the introduction of mobile digital platforms. This study believes that the development of digital finance can alleviate the information scarcity caused by geographical challenges, improving rural financial infrastructure at a lower cost. The following sections will use the rapidly expanding mobile digital platform and the ongoing development of the rural household credit network as examples to illustrate the advantages of digital technology in promoting rural financial development.
I. Promotion of Mobile Digital Platforms:

1. Promotion of Information Exchange through Mature Technology: One of the fundamental issues causing the underdevelopment of rural finance is the lack of information due to the characteristics of natural villages in rural areas, which are often small, scattered, and remote. This leads to difficulties in establishing rural financial infrastructure, resulting in inadequate information flow between both the supply and demand sides of financial services. To address the information scarcity caused by these geographical challenges, it is essential to find a means of information exchange

that is not restricted by geographical location. Mobile digital technology, which is not constrained by geography, facilitates communication and information exchange between points and multiple locations at any distance. Moreover, rural wireless communication networks have achieved nearly full coverage, and mobile payment technology is relatively mature. These factors can significantly enhance information exchange in rural financial services. Given the rapid development of information technology, a global information revolution is underway. By promoting the use of mobile digital platforms in financial services, we can enable rural residents to be a part of this information revolution, gradually digitizing their lives in rural areas and enriching financial services related to agriculture.

Reduction of Costs in Non-Physical Platforms: The cost and convenience of providing and accessing financial services through mobile digital platforms are significantly lower than traditional physical platforms. In terms of service provision, there is no need to establish physical financial branches. Instead, services are offered through self-service platforms, saving expenses related to leasing physical spaces, equipment wear and tear, and staff salaries. The cost of mobile digital platforms is approximately 85% lower than that of traditional counter transactions. Additionally, rural residents can access convenient financial services from the comfort of their homes, eliminating the need for complicated transportation and long queues. Furthermore, digital financial services not only include the "big three" rural financial services like savings, transfers, and remittances but also offer a diverse range of services such as online shopping, wealth management, mutual fund investments, crowdfunding, etc. This diversification reduces transaction costs, enhances information transparency and symmetry, and allows rural residents to access a broader range of financial services at lower costs.

Rapid Promotion Leading to the Transformation of Rural Financial Service Models: China's rural areas are already well-equipped to promote mobile digital financial platforms. In terms of mobile digital financial platform clients, rural areas have high and rapidly growing rates of digital penetration. The number of rural mobile internet users in China is increasing annually, and the conditions for expanding mobile digital platforms to clients are already in place. On the supply side, traditional financial institutions are actively transitioning to digital networks. Large state-owned banks such as Industrial and Commercial Bank and China Merchants Bank have already established digital customer service platforms. Furthermore, with the rapid proliferation of smartphones and digital technology in rural areas, the rural financial services market is expanding. Agricultural financial products are becoming more innovative in terms of targeting, breadth, and depth of services, prompting traditional financial institutions like rural credit cooperatives to transition. The rural financial industry is entering a period of rapid development, driving the transformation of rural financial service models, which, in turn, provides strong support for rural economic development and national urbanization efforts.

In summary, mobile digital platforms serve as the foundation of rural finance with mature technology, lower costs, and ease of promotion. Although the development of rural financial infrastructure in China has received significant support from the government, there are still many deficiencies. Mobile digital financial platforms are needed as a complementary solution. Furthermore, to promote inclusive rural economic development and ensure the synchronous development of rural and urban finance, the establishment of mobile digital financial platforms is essential.
II. Establishment of Rural Household Credit Networks:

In China, the traditional credit reporting system is primarily led by the People's Bank of China. Currently, there are credit reporting systems for both enterprises and individuals. However, these systems only cover a limited group of people who have had credit relationships with banks. The coverage is narrow, and the credit records are incomplete. Notably, 23% of the data in these systems come from urban residents, leaving a significant portion of the rural population unaccounted for. As of the end of 2015, only around 160 million rural households had established credit profiles, with more than a billion creditworthy rural residents remaining without recognized credit records. This inadequacy of the People's Bank of China's credit reporting system is closely related to the delayed development of rural credit environments and the frequent occurrence of "difficulty in obtaining loans" in rural areas.

Insufficient information is a key factor contributing to difficulties in financing for rural households and making it costlier. Financial institutions are either unable to access the credit information of such customers or find the cost of obtaining it prohibitively high, leading to reluctance in extending loans. The fundamental problem in developing rural finance is the issue of information. The critical solution to this problem, facilitated by digital technology, lies in the application of big data. The establishment of a rural credit reporting network can help drive the improvement of rural financial infrastructure.

Specifically, the development of digital technology can assist in consolidating scattered information about rural households, reducing the cost of information retrieval. Big data credit reporting incorporates a portion of the population not covered by the People's Bank of China's credit system, significantly addressing the shortcomings of traditional credit reporting in terms of coverage. Rural digital financial institutions use big data technology to gather information about rural residents in the digital realm, record their behavioral patterns, and conduct comprehensive analyses to more accurately assess their repayment capacity and willingness. This approach rectifies the severe insufficiency in the rural credit reporting system. Presently, China's big data credit reporting systems primarily fall into three categories:
E-commerce Platforms: These platforms act as third-party organizations that provide their credit data to other companies for a fee, engaging in commercial credit reporting.

P2P (Peer-to-Peer) Platforms' Proprietary Credit Systems: Various P2P platforms create their own credit systems, mainly using digital technology and their designed assessment models to offer reference points for their credit operations.

Third-Party Credit Reporting Platforms for the Same Industry: This type of platform complements the People's Bank of China's credit system. For instance, Shanghai Zhima Credit Information Co., Ltd., under the People's Bank of China, has developed the Network Financial Credit Sharing System (NFCS). Such platforms are designed to establish information-sharing systems. These systems collect customer information from both sides of P2P lending platforms, then integrate and process the data to provide standardized credit products for participating organizations. These platforms contribute to mitigating information asymmetry between lenders and
borrowers, thereby averting credit risk.

In contrast to traditional credit data sources, big data credit reporting is more extensive, with lower costs for acquiring and processing data. This significantly reduces loan risk and costs, enabling more rural customers to access loan funds, ultimately promoting inclusive rural finance. Commercial banks already possess vast amounts of data, but this data mainly pertains to structured information, such as customers' accounts, fund transfers, assets, liabilities, etc. This data only allows for the assessment of customers' repayment capacity but does not gauge their willingness to repay. Rural financial institutions can tap into unstructured information about rural customers, with rural credit cooperatives, rural commercial banks, customer managers, and agents entering this information into digital systems. This unstructured information encompasses aspects that are difficult to put into words, such as who may be more likely to default or engage in dishonest financial practices. Modern digital technology is essential for extracting information from non-structured data sources, including call records, social network chat information, audio recordings of customer manager visits, customer service audio, branch video, online banking records, and e-commerce transaction traces.

These initiatives can significantly contribute to enhancing rural credit information and, in turn, help bridge the information gap in rural finance, ultimately facilitating the development of rural finance in China.
Building a big data credit reporting network in rural areas, based on the existing information infrastructure, can involve expanding information collection in four key areas:

Integration with Social Networking Platforms and E-commerce Platforms: This allows for the collection of customer behavioral information. Behavioral data can provide insights into a customer's willingness to repay, which traditional financial institutions' credit reporting can't assess, as it typically focuses on repayment capacity.

Integration of Information from Various Government Departments: This includes data retained by government departments such as Industry and Commerce, Taxation, the Court, the Procuratorate, Public Security, Political and Legal Affairs Committees, and Family Planning Commissions.
Integration of Records from Utility Providers: This involves integrating data related to water, electricity, gas, telecommunications operators, and hospitals, among others.

Leveraging the Role of Agents in Rural Areas: Make use of various institutions, such as China Mobile, China Telecom, China Unicom, and village clinics, as agents. Encourage them to input rural households' information into the system.

Additionally, the process through which rural households obtain basic financial services and financing services is also a means for them to learn about financial knowledge and enhance their creditworthiness. Digital development can contribute to improving the financial education level of rural households and fostering credit awareness. On one hand, channels like mobile banking, online banking, and mobile apps can be used to disseminate relevant financial knowledge. On the other hand, social networks can play a significant role. For instance, banks can assess a borrower's creditworthiness by referencing their social network information. Furthermore, many young farmers use communication apps like QQ, WeChat, and Weibo to exchange information. Regulatory authorities can use these social networks to share basic
financial knowledge. This helps rural households gain a better understanding of financial concepts.
7.1.2 Lowering the Cost of Rural Financial Services

The primary reason for the weak capacity of rural economic and financial services is the high cost, making it difficult for financial institutions on the supply side to reach the "threshold" of providing services with sufficient profits. As a result, the majority of rural households are excluded from financial services, missing out on financial inclusion. Specifically, digital finance can reduce the cost of rural financial services from two dimensions.
Aggregating Multiple Financial Services into One:

Rural financial service demands are characterized by a large volume of low-cost individual transactions. This characteristic leads to high supply costs in rural financial services. Economically, this type of market demand is defined as a "long-tail market" or a "niche market," where the cost can be minimized by using a large sales base to provide affordable products or services. This approach is impossible for traditional manual operations but is feasible under digital technology conditions. The practice of digital finance in rural areas has proven its feasibility.

At the technical level, digital finance operates through a network of physical components such as computers, network cables, switches, and routers. The most basic unit of computation is the computer, which is equipped with the power to process information efficiently. In the information revolution, the speed of processing various information by computer programs far exceeds human capabilities. Another advantage is its strong aggregation ability. Different types of financial service products cannot be combined for humans. However, for a computer, these are different software execution processes, ultimately processing in binary, and they can be combined easily. Thus, computers have significantly faster processing speeds and stronger aggregation capabilities, the more data they need to handle. For financial services, a single computer system's one-second processing capacity can be equivalent to a person's day's worth of work or even more. As long as the program is strictly written according to a fixed process, with timely and reliable information collection, the computer information system can effectively and quickly complete the work. With China's rural "Triple Network Convergence" project, we can be confident that digital finance terminals will achieve full coverage in rural areas. When various mobile customer terminals in rural digital finance submit service request information for various types of loan services, such as weddings, funerals, and others, to the digital financial service server computer in the specified format, the computer works according to the established software system's process. It can simultaneously serve many or even a vast number of customers, and, for the computer, the transaction cost of these many customers applying for service is almost the same as a single customer.

Additionally, digital technology exhibits a strong aggregation effect, powerful enough to allow people all over the world to see the same information or send information to the same place. Given the vast rural population in China, suppose tens of thousands of people apply for financial services through digital technology simultaneously, each person applying for one thousand yuan. In that case, for the computer, this transaction is one billion yuan, and the service cost is nearly identical to an individual applying for one billion yuan.
Reducing the Cost of Single Rural Financial Services:

Digital technology can effectively address the challenge of high transaction costs in rural financial services, primarily by reducing the costs caused by "distance" through mobile digital payments. Digital information integration and analysis technology lowers the costs caused by difficulties in information retrieval. Digital resource allocation benefits the profitability prospects of rural-related industries, reducing the costs of risk assessment and collateral risk management. Digital technology plays a significant role in social resource allocation, acting as a bridge between the market and planning. It introduces planning into the market, and planning fulfills the market. Rural products and collateral assets can go online, enabling them to directly connect with the market, enhancing the profitability prospects of rural-related industries, and reducing the costs of risk assessment and collateral risk management. Furthermore, transactions conducted on online platforms leave behind transaction information, which can serve as the primary source of customer assessment for financial service providers. In a well-informed environment, loans and financial services may not even require collateral.

In conclusion, digital technology can significantly reduce the cost of rural financial services, thus improving the competitiveness of rural customers in obtaining financial services and allowing financial service providers to achieve sustainable development.
7.1.3 Enhancing the Accessibility of Rural Financial Markets for Financing

Rural financial markets suffer from limited service providers, leading to a lack of competitive dynamics and restricting the development of the financing side in rural finance. Currently, large banks possess substantial strength, making it challenging for smaller financial institutions to challenge them. However, digital finance has the capability to capture market share, urging traditional financial institutions to actively participate in competition in the rural financial market, thus increasing the availability of financing. This leads to a more dynamic market and an elevation in the quality of financial products and services through innovation.
In comparison to traditional rural financial institutions, digital finance boasts three key advantages:

First, it aligns closely with the digital economy and offers 'small yet beautiful' products. The digital economy encompasses a series of commercial activities conducted in the digital sphere. Currently, the digital economy is experiencing rapid growth, and e-commerce is significantly impacting traditional markets and trade fairs. As the financial industry is intertwined with and services the economy, the development of the digital economy promotes the growth of digital finance. Digital financial service providers can easily analyze a customer's profit potential and market opportunities through digital transaction records and other information. Additionally, obtaining financial services such as loans is made easy, quick, and convenient compared to the complex processes of traditional financial services.

Second, it utilizes digital technology as a production tool, which results in high production efficiency and rapid dissemination. As mentioned earlier, digital technology's powerful aggregation and batch processing capabilities make digital finance highly efficient in its operations. It has low production costs, extensive market coverage, and leverages 'word of mouth' to transmit information, leading to an explosive impact. Therefore, it can efficiently produce and quickly capture market
share.

Third, it leverages the advantages of digital resource allocation. Digital finance can reconfigure resources through the vast digital marketplace, using products produced in rural industries with lower sales volumes or assets like land and property as collateral. This process enhances the efficiency of resource recycling, encourages the development of rural-related industries, and significantly reduces the non-performing asset ratio compared to traditional rural financial services.
7.1.4 Enhance Rural Financial Regulatory Capacity

Economic activities must be conducted under regulation, and unregulated markets, especially financial markets, are prone to social issues. Robust regulation is key to addressing the challenges in rural financial policies, and enhancing China's rural financial regulatory capacity is crucial. It's feasible to enhance rural financial regulatory capacity through digital means for the following reasons:

1.Regulation is based on information, and digital technology excels in information collection and processing, enabling remote monitoring by professionals. Government agencies rely on information for financial regulation. Digital tools can efficiently collect crucial information and, through batch processing by professionals and information systems, enable remote monitoring of financial institutions at the grassroots level. Additionally, collaboration with local regulatory authorities allows for timely information verification, enhancing the regulatory capabilities of local offices.

2.Digital applications promote transparency, enabling social oversight. Digital applications have strong transparency characteristics. Transactions by some new financial companies can be displayed online. If customers notice issues with a particular internet financial company's operations, they can publish this information online. Regulatory authorities can then investigate with specialized tools to determine if there is a genuine problem. If issues exist, the authority can order corrective actions. If not, the misinformation is dispelled. Public engagement has the potential to produce significant regulatory effects.

Mobile digital technologies, in particular, can play a vital role in rural financial regulation. With mobile phones moving toward real-name registration, phone numbers can serve as identity credentials. Users can store value on their mobile phones, effectively creating virtual accounts tied to their phone numbers. These numbers function similarly to deposit accounts and can be accessed from anywhere at any time. With access to call logs, contacts, and text messages, mobile service providers have insight into users' core social networks. This information can be used to evaluate borrowers' creditworthiness and facilitate the monitoring of credit activities. Moreover, mobile operators can use their networks for "social punishment" (e.g., suspending communication services) to control default and moral hazards among borrowers and their agents (e.g., institutions), effectively supervising both individual borrowers and institutional operations.

In summary, a combination of remote and local supervision, automated and manual regulation, professional and public oversight can bolster regulatory capabilities. This approach allows rural digital finance to develop under effective regulatory oversight.
7.2 The Application Model of Digital Finance in Rural Financial Development

With government policy support, rural digital inclusive finance has been developing rapidly. The Central Document No. 1 of 2016 emphasized the need to "accelerate the construction of a multi-level, widely covered, and sustainable rural financial service system, develop rural inclusive finance, reduce financing costs, and comprehensively activate the rural financial service chain." It also included "Internet finance" for the first time and proposed to "guide the standardized development of internet finance and mobile finance in rural areas." Strong policy support from the government will drive more digital financial resources into rural areas, injecting new vitality into the development of rural inclusive finance.

In the Central Document No. 1 of 2017, it was mentioned that there should be a "fast-paced rural financial innovation to ensure the continued growth of loans to 'three rural' sectors." It emphasized "encouraging financial institutions to actively use digital technology to provide small-scale deposits and loans, payment settlements, and insurance services for agricultural entities." The 19th National Congress report put forward the "Rural Revitalization Strategy," which emphasized "prioritizing the development of agriculture and rural areas" and establishing a mechanism and policy system for integrated urban and rural development according to the overall requirements of "prosperous industries, eco-friendly living, cultural advancement, effective governance, and improved living standards."

In the Central Document No. 1 of 2018, there was an emphasis on "promoting the upgrading of rural infrastructure," including the acceleration of infrastructure construction such as rural roads, water supply, gas supply, environmental protection, power grids, logistics, information, broadcasting, and television. It suggested focusing on areas that would benefit from infrastructure upgrades like public roads, power grids, construction machinery, and pipelines.

Starting in 2014, digital finance began to gain momentum in rural areas and gradually expanded its strategic presence in rural areas through various forms. Alibaba and JD Group were among the first companies to promote e-commerce in rural areas and expand rural digital financial services. Subsequently, many other enterprises and digital financial platforms entered rural areas. Leading agricultural companies like Dabeinong Group, New Hope Group, Yilongdai, Yixin, and various P2P platforms started venturing into rural markets in different ways. This significantly promoted the development of rural digital finance, filling the gap left by traditional financial institutions in serving rural development.

In the process of development, these enterprises not only drove the growth of the agricultural economy, providing essential support for accelerating rural development, but also promoted their own business growth. It was a win-win process. Several fixed application models have gradually emerged during the development of digital finance in rural financial development, as shown in Table 7.1. These models represent the financial transformation by digital enterprises in the first three categories, while the fourth one represents the digitization of traditional financial institutions implementing "digital + rural finance.”
Table 7.1 The Application Models of Digital Finance in Rural Financial Development
Application Models

7.2.1 Financial Technology Companies Exploring Practical Applications.

As China's urban e-commerce market gradually approaches saturation, major e-commerce platforms have expanded their reach into rural areas, focusing on second and third-tier cities after consolidating their presence in first-tier cities. Comprehensive e-commerce platforms, represented by Alibaba and JD, have implemented strategies to penetrate rural markets through rural e-commerce channel expansion. This move has filled numerous gaps in rural financial services. Table 7.2 provides a comparison of Alibaba and JD's strategies in the rural financial market. It is evident that both Alibaba and JD leverage rural digital infrastructure. Alibaba uses Rural Taobao as a carrier with payment at its core, while JD centers its strategy around logistics. Despite their different approaches, both companies have not only achieved profitability but also promoted the development of rural inclusive finance.
